title: LDAP password exposed in URL query parameter on GET /authentication/authenticateUsingGet
description: The authenticateUsingGet endpoint accepts the LDAP password as a URL query parameter (@QueryParam("ps")). Query parameters are written in plain text to server access logs, browser history, HTTP referrer headers, and network proxies. An attacker with access to any of these can harvest credentials without network interception.
beforeCode: public Response authenticateUsingGet(@QueryParam("uN") String uN, @QueryParam("ps") String ps)
afterCode:
// Move to POST. Accept credentials in request body or Authorization header only.
// @POST public Response authenticate(@Context HttpServletRequest request) { String[] creds = readAuthorizationHeader(request.getHeader("Authorization")); ... }

title: All authenticated users receive identical hardcoded API key from getCinUser()
description: getCinUser() unconditionally sets cinuser.setApiKey("1448a8f83def4b839c9d1460046e45dd") for every authenticated user. This means every user who successfully authenticates receives the same API key UUID in the response, granting any user the same access privileges regardless of their identity or role. The static analyzer flagged API_KEY at line 80 (the constant name), but missed this runtime assignment that exposes the key to all callers.
afterCode:
// Retrieve the actual per-consumer API key from the Consumer entity or generate a unique per-session token.
// cinuser.setApiKey(consumerService.getApiKeyForUser(ldapUser.getUid()));

title: Mutable static HashMap roleMap written inside authenticate() causes race condition
description: AuthentiationServiceImpl declares 'private static Map<String, List<String>> roleMap = new HashMap<>()' and then writes to it inside authenticate() on every call: roleMap.put(ADMIN, ...) and roleMap.put(READER, ...). HashMap is not thread-safe. Under concurrent authentication requests, simultaneous put() operations corrupt the map's internal state (null entries, infinite loops in older JDKs), and one thread may read partial state written by another. Static analysis cannot detect this because the write happens at runtime through service method calls.

title: Static mutable StatusCode.responseCode bleeds between concurrent CORVET requests
description: StatusCode holds 'private static String responseCode' with a static setter. CorvetClientServiceImpl.setTimeout() calls StatusCode.setResponseCode("206") on timeout, and callToCovertWithSetTimeout() immediately reads StatusCode.getResponseCode() on the next line. Under concurrent VIN lookups, thread A's timeout sets the code to "206", and thread B (which did not time out) reads "206" and returns a false timeout response to its caller. This is invisible to static analysis because the hazard spans two methods and a shared class.
afterCode:
// Remove static state entirely. Return the response code directly from setTimeout() as a method return value or a dedicated result object.
// public String callToCovertWithSetTimeout(...) { String statusCode = setTimeout(httpClient, httpPost, timeoutValue, "Corvet"); ... }

title: SSL certificate validation fully disabled for all CORVET HTTPS calls
description: callToCovert() and callToCovertWithSetTimeout() in CorvetClientServiceImpl both construct an SSLContext with a TrustManager whose checkServerTrusted() and checkClientTrusted() methods have empty bodies (accept any certificate) and pair it with NoopHostnameVerifier.INSTANCE. Every HTTPS call to CORVET is therefore vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks — an attacker on the network path can present any certificate and intercept or modify VIN data. The static analyzer reported long methods but has no rule to detect semantically empty TrustManager implementations.
afterCode:
// Remove the custom TrustManager. Use the JVM default trust store:
// CloseableHttpClient httpClient = HttpClients.custom().setDefaultCredentialsProvider(credsProvider).build();

title: SSL certificate validation fully disabled for all THub HTTPS calls in AlcdvServiceImpl
description: AlcdvServiceImpl.getHttpClient() creates an SSLContext with 'TrustStrategy acceptingTrustStrategy = (certificate, authType) -> true' and uses NoopHostnameVerifier.INSTANCE. This lambda always returns true, accepting any certificate regardless of issuer or validity. All HTTPS calls to the THub external service are exposed to MITM attacks. This is a distinct instance from the CORVET finding — both services independently disable SSL validation at the same time.
afterCode:
// Remove the custom TrustStrategy. Trust only the server certificate pinned to the THub CA:
// sslContext = SSLContexts.custom().loadTrustMaterial(new File(appConfig.getTrustStorePath()), appConfig.getTrustStorePassword().toCharArray()).build();

title: new Timer() per request creates unbounded daemon threads in CorvetClientServiceImpl.setTimeout()
description: setTimeout() calls 'new Timer(true).schedule(task, timeOutToSet)' creating a new java.util.Timer (which spawns a new daemon thread) on every invocation. Under high VIN lookup load — 100 simultaneous requests — 100 threads are created outside of any managed thread pool. These threads are never explicitly shut down. On sustained load this exhausts available threads and destabilises the JVM. A static rule-based analyser cannot model per-call timer lifecycle.

title: Identical TrustManager SSL-bypass block duplicated verbatim in callToCovert() and callToCovertWithSetTimeout()
description: callToCovert() (line 100) and callToCovertWithSetTimeout() (line 183) each contain a complete copy of the same ~50-line block: TrustManager[] creation with empty checkServerTrusted/checkClientTrusted, SSLContext init, NoopHostnameVerifier, proxy credentials, and HTTP POST setup. The static analyzer flagged both as 'long method' but did not identify the duplication. Any security fix to the SSL bypass (AI-005) must be applied identically in both methods — the current structure makes it likely that a partial fix will leave one path still insecure.
beforeCode:
// callToCovert() lines 100-170 and callToCovertWithSetTimeout() lines 183-260
// contain a verbatim copy of TrustManager[], SSLContext, proxy setup, and HttpPost execution
afterCode:
// Extract a private buildHttpClient(CredentialsProvider) method that both callToCovert() and
// callToCovertWithSetTimeout() call. SSL fix applied once in the shared method.

title: HS256 shared key is identical for all consumers enabling cross-consumer JWT forgery
description: generateSharedKeyToken() signs JWTs using 'new MACSigner(AuthConstant.SECRET_KEY.getBytes())' where SECRET_KEY = "sample byte string for share key". This single key is used for all consumers who do not require RSA signing. Any consumer (or attacker who has read any token) can forge a valid JWT for a different consumer by re-signing a modified payload with the same known key. The static analyzer found the hardcoded string (D11-001) but could not detect the architectural risk that all 'shared key' consumers share an identical signing secret.

title: Arbitrary caller-supplied JWT claims accepted without whitelist in POST /authentication/token
description: generate() passes the raw request body 'information' directly into addStandardClaims(): jsonPayload is parsed from the caller-supplied string and then standard claims (iss, iat, exp) are added on top. Any caller with a valid API key can inject arbitrary claims into the JWT payload — including 'role', 'admin', 'scope', or any application-specific claim — because no claim whitelist or sanitisation is applied before signing. Downstream services trusting these claims are exposed to privilege escalation.

title: processFile() performs 346-line multi-step DB write without @Transactional — partial imports on failure
description: FileServiceImpl.processFile() (346 lines, no @Transactional) iterates file records and commits them incrementally via insert(). If the method throws mid-file — due to a DB constraint, network reset, or unchecked exception — all rows processed before the failure point are permanently committed while the remainder are not. The resulting partially-imported dataset is silently inconsistent with the source file. The static analyzer flagged the method length but cannot reason about transactional boundaries.

title: Deprecated GET /authentication/authenticateUsingGet has no rate limiting enabling brute-force LDAP attacks
description: authenticateUsingGet() is marked @Deprecated but remains reachable. It calls authentiationService.authenticate(uN, ps) against LDAP on every request with no throttle, lockout, or CAPTCHA. An attacker can enumerate valid LDAP usernames and enumerate passwords at the rate the LDAP server allows — potentially thousands of attempts per second. The static analyzer has no concept of missing runtime controls such as rate limiting or fail2ban integration.
afterCode:
// Either remove the endpoint entirely (preferred — it is deprecated).
// Or add a Bucket4j / Resilience4j rate-limiter: @RateLimiter(name = "auth") before delegating to the service.
